claimText: Musa indandamanensis is a species of wild banana discovered in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.
evidenceExcerpt: First recorded from a remote tropical forest near Krishna Nala reserve forest in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands in 2012.
examUse: Statement-based question on endemic or recently discovered species locations.

claimText: Musa indandamanensis has the longest recorded infructescence among banana species, measuring about 4.2 metres.
evidenceExcerpt: An infructescence of about 4.2 metres recorded in species of wild banana Musa indandamanensis, making it the longest infructescence recorded in bananas.
examUse: Fact-based statement in a multi-statement question regarding unique plant species.

claimText: The Musa indandamanensis plant grows up to 11 metres high and features unique green flowers.
evidenceExcerpt: Unique green flowers; fruit bunch lux (axis) thrice size of regular banana species. 11 metres high.
examUse: Identification question based on unique botanical features.

claimText: Musa indandamanensis serves as a natural genetic resource for developing high-yielding and disease-resistant banana varieties.
evidenceExcerpt: Natural genetic resource for plant breeders to develop high yielding and disease resistant varieties.
examUse: Application or significance statement in a question about wild crop relatives and genetic resources.
